setup is a bit more involved, so let me write it down.

So what's SSH? SSH stands for "Secure Shell" — a cryptographic network protocol. Back in the early days of the web, we used HTTP, which is plaintext transport. Plaintext transport has obvious security issues — for example, if someone intercepts your packets, they can grab your username and password. SSH was created to solve this.
SSH uses asymmetric encryption for authentication. That means there are two files: a

. What's asymmetric encryption? Symmetric encryption means both sender and receiver use the same key. Asymmetric uses a public/private key pair — the public key verifies that your private key is legit, so only you (the private key holder) can decrypt or connect.
It stays on your machine and your machine only.

Now let's generate a GitHub-friendly SSH key. How do we know what GitHub wants? GitHub's docs cover it in
Generating a new SSH key and adding it to the ssh-agent
You'll notice this is different from what most online articles say (
). The RSA version is for older systems that don't support Ed25519. Ed25519's been around a while and has better security and performance, so since GitHub recommends Ed25519, that's what we'll use.
After running it, it'll ask where to save the key and whether you want to set a passphrase:

Generating public/private ed25519 key pair.
Enter file in which to save the key (/Users/rayxu/.ssh/id_ed25519):
Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):
Enter file in which to save the key (/Users/rayxu/.ssh/id_ed25519):
If you don't need anything special, just hit Enter through the prompts. When you see this screen, the key's generated:
Getting the SSH public key and adding it to GitHub
Head to the path it just showed you. For me that's
folder is hidden by default — press Shift + Command +
(the period key) to show hidden folders.
. You'll see what looks like random gibberish — select it all, copy it. Then head to GitHub Settings.
In the sidebar, find "SSH and GPG keys".
At the top of this page is SSH keys — and next to that is a "New SSH key" button. Click it.
(I literally deleted my own SSH key just to write this post...)
You'll land on an Add new page. The
is whatever you want — I usually use my computer name. The
field goes the public key you just copied.
You might get asked for your credentials again. After that, you'll see your new key on the SSH keys page.
At this point you're basically done, but the little key icon isn't lit up yet. Pick any private repo and try cloning it via SSH:
If it clones cleanly, you're good.
The key icon on the SSH keys page should light up too — meaning the key's actively in use.
Hi hsiangfeng! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
If it errors out, just walk through the steps again.
